content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Chairman Larson 
Date 
March 16, 1987 
File No. M-87-089 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Bob Leidi~i:25 
Subject: Reporting of Country Club Golfing Passes Under New Gift 
Regulations 
You have inquired regarding the correct method for valuing 
and reporting gift passes received from private country clubs 
for golfing privileges. 
As I understand the facts, the country 
club issues an annual pass to the public official. 
The 
official may then utilize the pass for golfing at no charge. 
The country club keeps a record of the usage and reports the 
value of the actual usage to the public official at the end of 
the year. 
Under newly adopted Regulation 18726.3, the value is the 
actual use made of the pass by the official any any guests he 
brings in under the pass, plus any possible use by anyone to 
whom he transfers (loans) the pass. 
As I understand it, here there was no transfer. 
Hence, the 
value reported by the country club should accurately reflect 
the value of the gift received. 
If that value is $50 or more, 
it must be reported on the official's annual statement of 
economic interests. 
If the value was less than $50, no 
reporting is required. 
BL:plh

sections.question: You have inquired regarding the correct method for valuing and reporting gift passes received from private country clubs for golfing privileges.
sections.conclusion: Under newly adopted Regulation 18726.3, the value is the actual use made of the pass by the official and any guests he brings in under the pass, plus any possible use by anyone to whom he transfers (loans) the pass. If that value is $50 or more, it must be reported on the official's annual statement of economic interests. If the value was less than $50, no reporting is required.
sections.question_synthetic: How should public officials value and report gift passes from private country clubs used for golfing under the new gift regulations?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The value of a country club golfing pass is determined by actual usage of the pass by the official and any guests, and must be reported on the annual statement of economic interests if the value is $50 or more.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Memorandum format rather than traditional advice letter format, but contains clear question and conclusion sections. No formal section headers but content clearly distinguishes inquiry from response.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have inquired regarding the correct method for valuing and reporting gift passes received from private country clubs for golfing privileges.

CONCLUSION: Under newly adopted Regulation 18726.3, the value is the actual use made of the pass by the official and any guests he brings in under the pass, plus any possible use by anyone to whom he transfers (loans) the pass. If that value is $50 or more, it must be reported on the official's annual statement of economic interests. If the value was less than $50, no reporting is required.

As I understand the facts, the country club issues an annual pass to the public official. The official may then utilize the pass for golfing at no charge. The country club keeps a record of the usage and reports the value of the actual usage to the public official at the end of the year. Under newly adopted Regulation 18726.3, the value is the actual use made of the pass by the official any any guests he brings in under the pass, plus any possible use by anyone to whom he transfers (loans) the pass. As I understand it, here there was no transfer. Hence, the value reported by the country club should accurately reflect the value of the gift received. embedding.summary: FPPC advice on valuing and reporting gift country club golfing passes under new Regulation 18726.3, based on actual usage with a $50 reporting threshold.
